current_fact.fact_text: The Norway Debate began in Britain’s House of Commons on May 7, 1940, as MPs fiercely criticized the government’s mishandling of the Norwegian campaign. The debate’s fallout toppled Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain and paved the way for Winston Churchill to become wartime leader.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

current_fact.match_reason: A stormy parliamentary debate that ushered in a new leader aligns with an episode about weathering transitions and finding hope at the edge of one chapter and the start of another.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1915, the German U-boat U-20 torpedoed the British ocean liner RMS Lusitania off the coast of Ireland, killing 1,199 passengers and crew, including 128 Americans. The sinking shocked public opinion, became a symbol of unrestricted submarine warfare, and nudged the United States closer to entering World War I.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The Lusitania tragedy became a grim lesson in the consequences of technology and warfare; pairing it with an episode about learning underscores how collective shocks can reshape political decisions and public consciousness.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1915, the Beiyang government of the Republic of China reluctantly acceded to 13 of Japan’s notorious Twenty-One Demands, granting Tokyo sweeping economic and political influence over Manchuria and parts of China’s economy. The move sparked outrage at home and abroad, fueling nationalist resentment that would echo for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: An unequal set of demands imposed on China and an episode about a fiercely political artist both spotlight resistance, power imbalances, and the long echo of imposed control.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1794, Maximilien Robespierre stood before France’s National Convention to introduce the Cult of the Supreme Being, a deistic state religion meant to replace Catholicism during the French Revolution. The festival he envisioned blended civic ritual, philosophy, and carefully choreographed symbolism in an attempt to engineer virtue through belief.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A revolutionary attempt to craft a new state religion and an episode exploring Flamenco’s evolving rituals and spiritual intensity both reveal how culture, belief, and performance intertwine.

queue.fact_text: HMS Victory, the 104-gun ship that would later serve as Admiral Nelson’s flagship at the Battle of Trafalgar, was launched on May 7, 1765 at Chatham Dockyard in Kent. Curiously, she sat in reserve for over a decade before finally being commissioned, a sleeping giant of oak and canvas awaiting her role in naval history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: The launch of a legendary sailing warship and an episode centered on maritime journeys across the Mediterranean both invite us onto decks where history, sea, and song meet.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1920, Polish forces under Józef Piłsudski captured Kyiv during the Kyiv offensive of the Polish–Soviet War, briefly installing a friendly government. Within weeks, however, Red Army counterattacks swept them back, turning the city into a revolving door of control in a fluid, brutal conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A turbulent Polish military campaign and an episode built around personal travels through Poland are joined by geography, allowing a war-torn past and a curious present to resonate across the same map.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1999, during the Kosovo War, a NATO aircraft mistakenly bombed the Chinese embassy in Belgrade, killing three Chinese journalists and wounding 20 people. The strike triggered a diplomatic crisis and public protests in China, highlighting how the fog of war can spill far beyond the intended battlefield.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: An errant bomb that caused international outrage and an episode about a band whose entire catalog is a critique of militarism and power both channel anger at the unintended casualties of conflict.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 2004, American contractor Nick Berg was kidnapped in Iraq and beheaded by militants, who recorded the killing on video and released it online. It became one of the earliest widely circulated examples of digital terror propaganda, foreshadowing how the internet would transform the theater of war and horror.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A brutal act amplified by early online video and an episode about our fraught dependence on digital devices both grapple with how the internet magnifies human extremes, from fear to connection.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1940, the Norway Debate also marked a rare moment when parliamentary criticism directly toppled a sitting British prime minister during wartime. The vote revealed a fracture between political leadership and public expectations, showing how even in crisis, governments can be forced to change course.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A government forced to rethink its leadership during war neatly parallels an episode devoted to the humility and disruption that come with changing one’s mind under pressure.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1765, Chatham Dockyard launched HMS Victory, whose hull and timbers represented some of the finest shipbuilding of the age. Designed as a first-rate ship of the line, she carried more than 100 guns and thousands of trees within her frame, a floating forest turned into a weapon of empire.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A monumental wooden warship launched from a dockyard and an evening voyage through global grooves both carry a sense of slow-building momentum, of journeys that start quiet and end in full sail.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 2025, Indian Army and Air Force units reportedly carried out coordinated strikes, codenamed Operation SINDOOR, against militant hideouts in Pakistan after a deadly attack in Pahalgam. Though details remain contested, the episode reflects how modern conflicts blend precision strikes, media narratives, and regional tension.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A tightly coordinated military operation and an episode about martial disciplines like Muay Thai both orbit around training, precision, and the fine line between controlled force and escalation.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1685, rebels clashed with Ottoman forces at the Battle of Vrtijeljka in what is now Montenegro, during the wider Great Turkish War. The engagement, fought on rugged terrain, saw local fighters and Venetian allies challenging imperial power along the edge of the Adriatic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A mountain battle between local rebels and an empire pairs naturally with an episode about desert peoples whose music is steeped in stories of resistance, landscape, and survival at the margins.

queue.fact_text: On May 7, 1915, the same year as the Lusitania tragedy, the Republic of China’s acceptance of most of Japan’s Twenty-One Demands contributed to a broader wave of anti-imperialist sentiment across Asia. Students and intellectuals would later look back on it as a cautionary tale of what happens when a weakened state is forced into unequal agreements.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: An unequal treaty that fueled anti-imperialist feeling in Asia parallels an episode on Afrobeat, a genre deeply tied to African struggles against colonialism and political domination.

queue.fact_text: By the time Space Shuttle Endeavour flew in 1992, the lineage that began with devices like Popov’s lightning detector had evolved into complex radar, telemetry, and communications networks woven into every mission. Each shuttle launch became a symphony of invisible signals, tracking, and feedback between Earth and orbit.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A shuttle mission orchestrated through invisible radio and data streams echoes an open-format evening mix where sound and mood drift freely through the ether, guided only by feeling and signal.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1992, Space Shuttle Endeavour lifted off on its maiden voyage, STS-49, a mission that included the dramatic in-orbit capture and repair of the stranded Intelsat VI satellite. Endeavour was built as the replacement for Challenger, carrying with it both technological refinement and the emotional weight of continuing NASA’s shuttle legacy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space shuttle’s first flight and an episode about re-framing emptiness as creative space both play with the idea of shifting perspectives and venturing into new frontiers.

published.fact_text: In 1895 in Saint Petersburg, Alexander Stepanovich Popov demonstrated a lightning detector that could capture and interpret electromagnetic waves, effectively building one of the world’s first primitive radio receivers. In parts of the former Soviet Union, this date is still celebrated as Radio Day, honoring the birth of wireless communication.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A pioneer of early radio and a show about deliberate, old-school listening both celebrate the roots of how we tune into invisible waves and give sound our full attention.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1824, Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony premiered in Vienna, introducing the now-iconic choral finale "Ode to Joy" to the world. Remarkably, the almost completely deaf composer stood on stage as the music unfolded around him, needing a gentle tap on the shoulder to turn and see the audience’s roaring applause he could no longer hear.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Beethoven’s sweeping, almost cinematic Ninth and an episode built around orchestral, neo-classical textures both tap into vast emotional landscapes that feel almost oceanic in scale.

published.fact_text: In 1952, British engineer Geoffrey Dummer publicly proposed the idea of the integrated circuit—tiny electronic components fabricated together on a single piece of semiconductor. His vision laid the conceptual groundwork for microchips, quietly setting the stage for everything from pocket calculators to smartphones and space probes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The birth of the microchip and an episode about our tangled relationship with smartphones are two chapters of the same story: how integrated circuits rewired our daily lives and inner worlds.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1946, a small Tokyo company called Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering was founded; it would later become Sony. Starting with repairing radios and making rice cookers that accidentally burned the rice, the firm eventually helped define modern electronics, from the Walkman to high-definition entertainment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: Sony’s role in popularizing audio tech and an episode on Auto-Tune both explore how companies and tools quietly reshape the way music is produced, polished, and heard.

published.fact_text: In 1930, the Salmas earthquake struck northwestern Iran and southeastern Turkey with a magnitude of 7.1, reaching a Mercalli intensity of IX and destroying numerous villages. The ground ruptured, homes collapsed, and up to 3,000 people lost their lives in a region already familiar with the restless force of tectonic plates.

published.match_reason: A devastating earthquake and an episode about weathering storms both revolve around the raw power of nature and the human search for steadiness when the ground literally or metaphorically shifts.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1986, Canadian climber Patrick Morrow became the first person to stand atop all Seven Summits—the highest peaks on each continent—from Everest to Vinson Massif. His achievement stitched together a planetary necklace of mountains climbed over years of cold, thin air and careful perseverance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A climber completing the Seven Summits and an episode dedicated to mountain landscapes both celebrate the strange mix of hardship and beauty found at high altitudes.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1960, Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev announced that his country had captured U.S. U-2 pilot Gary Powers, whose spy plane had been shot down deep inside Soviet territory. The revelation embarrassed the United States, shattered a carefully constructed cover story, and escalated Cold War tensions in the skies.

published.fact_text: In the early hours of May 7, 1945, a German submarine carried out the last U-boat attack of World War II, sinking two freighters off Scotland’s Firth of Forth. Within days, the U-boat campaign that had once terrorized the Atlantic fell silent as Germany capitulated.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: The final U-boat strike off the Scottish coast and an episode steeped in Scottish music both look to the same waters and skies, one through the lens of war’s end and the other through calm, reflective storytelling.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1942, during the Battle of the Coral Sea, American carrier-based aircraft located and sank the Japanese light carrier Shōhō. It was the first naval battle in history where opposing fleets fought without their ships ever seeing each other, trading blows entirely through aircraft and radio.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A sea battle fought beyond the horizon and an episode recorded from a sailboat both center the ocean as a stage—one for war’s innovation, the other for peaceful shared journeys.
